narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80—which is below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 38, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3rd out of 10, and your Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10, along with your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It predicted a 74.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and bar passage rates. This decision hinged on just a few standout factors from your profile that carried the most weight in the prediction. The biggest influence pushing toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 20% of your school—much lower than the average 5.5th decile—and signaled to the model a concerning drop-off in performance compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 3rd also pulled strongly in the failure direction, again below the average 5.7th decile, suggesting early struggles that persisted and outweighed other strengths for students like you. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 38—slightly above the 36.7 average—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing and showing the model some potential in your standardized testing ability. Overall, your grades in later undergrad years stood out as weaker than most admitted students who go on to pass the bar, while your LSAT provided a counterbalance but not enough to offset those gaps. Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your decile rankings are notably lower, placing you in the lower tail of the distribution where failure risks spike. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a white male from an upper-middle-income family—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger academic trajectories—but the model prioritizes patterns from historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your recent GPA through post-bac courses or a master's program, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your full-time commitment and background more heavily. With targeted improvements, you can reshape your profile for future applications and boost your bar passage odds.
